executiveSummary:
**Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis**

**Enrollment and Spending Trends**

National Medicaid enrollment reached 75.3 million beneficiaries as of January 2026, with widespread enrollment declines across major states reflecting the continued unwinding of pandemic-era continuous coverage protections. Most large states experienced enrollment reductions ranging from 1.8% to 7.6% year-over-year, with Michigan showing the steepest decline at 7.6%, while North Carolina was the notable exception with modest growth of 0.5%. Texas enrollment decreased 3.1% to 4.0 million enrollees, positioning it as the third-largest state program behind California and New York. National spending totaled $215.9 billion in fiscal year 2024, with a per-enrollee average of $2,869. Texas per-enrollee spending of $2,988 exceeded the national average by 4.2%, while maintaining relatively efficient spending compared to high-cost states like Pennsylvania ($4,377 per enrollee) and Massachusetts ($4,201 per enrollee). The federal government covered approximately 66% of total program costs nationally.

**Quality Performance and State Comparisons**

Quality measure performance reveals significant interstate variation, with national scores averaging 65.6 out of 100 points in 2024. High-performing states led by Massachusetts (81 points) and Minnesota (80 points) substantially outpace low-performing states, with Mississippi scoring just 48 points. Texas achieved a moderate quality score of 62 points, ranking 30th nationally and falling slightly below the national average, indicating opportunities for improvement in care coordination and outcomes measurement. The state's managed care penetration rate of 93.3% significantly exceeds the national average of 83.2%, covering 3.9 million of its 4.0 million enrollees through managed care arrangements. Recent federal regulatory activity includes proposed 2028 HCBS quality measures and generic information collection requirements, while ongoing HHS OIG oversight activities warrant continued monitoring for compliance implications across state programs.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Analysis

Texas operates the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with 4.0 million enrollees, representing a substantial 5.4% of national enrollment despite serving only 8.8% of the U.S. population. The state's enrollment declined 3.1% year-over-year, closely tracking the national deceleration as pandemic-era continuous enrollment provisions wind down. However, Texas faces distinct challenges as a non-expansion state, evident in its below-national-average per-enrollee spending of $2,988 compared to the national average of $2,869—though this figure masks the program's constrained eligibility that leaves many low-income adults without coverage. Among large states, Texas ranks competitively on cost efficiency, spending significantly less per enrollee than expansion states like New York ($3,431) and Pennsylvania ($4,377), while maintaining robust managed care penetration at 93.3%, well above the national average of 83.2%.

The program's most significant challenge lies in quality performance, where Texas scores 62 out of 100, ranking 30th nationally and falling into the "moderate" tier—a concerning position for such a large program. This places Texas behind peer southern states like North Carolina and below most large state comparators, suggesting systematic quality improvement opportunities. The state's heavy reliance on managed care, while cost-effective, requires enhanced oversight to ensure access and outcomes match the financial efficiency gains. Key areas to monitor include how Texas addresses quality gaps while maintaining its cost-conscious approach, particularly as federal regulatory activity intensifies around HCBS quality measures and generic data collection requirements that could impact state reporting burdens and performance metrics. The ongoing procurement activities visible through Texas HHSC suggest potential program modifications that could influence both cost and quality trajectories in the near term.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania Per-Enrollee Spending Significantly Above Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,377 is 53% higher than the national average of $2,868, indicating potential cost efficiency issues.

alerts.title: Florida Medicaid Spending Unusually Low Per Enrollee
alerts.description: Florida's per-enrollee spending of $2,031 is 29% below the national average despite having 3.6M enrollees, suggesting potential underfunding concerns.

alerts.title: Bottom 5 States Show Critically Poor Quality Performance
alerts.description: Five states (MS, OK, AL, SC, AR) scored below 55 on quality measures, significantly below the national average of 65.6.

alerts.title: Massachusetts High Quality Despite Very High Spending
alerts.description: Massachusetts combines the highest quality score (81) with very high per-enrollee spending ($4,201), suggesting potential best practices worth studying.

executiveInsights.title: TX Medicaid enrollment declining 3.1% YoY despite strong MC penetration
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.1% year-over-year to 4.03M enrollees, matching the national decline trend. However, TX maintains exceptionally high managed care penetration at 93.3% vs 83.2% national average.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment decline directly impacts contract revenue and volumes, while high MC penetration indicates strong system maturity that could face operational strain during unwinding periods.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review enrollment projections and MC capacity planning with HHSC leadership

executiveInsights.title: TX per-enrollee costs $2,988 exceed national average by $119
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,988 per Medicaid enrollee compared to the national average of $2,868, representing a 4.2% premium. Total TX expenditures reach $12.05B annually.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Above-average per-enrollee costs signal potential efficiency opportunities or higher-acuity populations that could drive additional contract value and optimization initiatives.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag cost efficiency analysis opportunities to HHSC budget leadership

executiveInsights.title: TX quality score ranks #30 nationally at 62/100, below improvement threshold
executiveInsights.summary: Texas quality performance at 62/100 places it in moderate tier, ranking 30th of 50 states. This falls below the high-performer threshold of 75 but above low-performer threshold of 60.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Mediocre quality rankings create risk for federal oversight and represent clear opportunity for Accenture to demonstrate value through quality improvement initiatives and performance consulting.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review quality improvement opportunities and benchmarking against top performers

executiveInsights.title: Multiple CMS regulatory changes targeting HCBS and data collection emerging
executiveInsights.summary: Federal Register shows active CMS rulemaking on 2028 HCBS Quality Measures and multiple Medicaid/CHIP data collection activities. Two separate information collection proposals indicate expanded reporting requirements.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: New regulatory requirements will drive implementation needs and compliance costs, creating potential expansion opportunities for Accenture's regulatory and systems capabilities.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag upcoming HCBS quality measures and data collection changes to compliance team

executiveInsights.title: HHS OIG maintaining active Medicaid oversight with current investigations
executiveInsights.summary: OIG signals show active Medicaid portfolio oversight and work plan items currently in progress. Multiple procurement portals for TX and comparison states remain actively monitored.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Active OIG oversight increases compliance scrutiny and audit risk, while also creating opportunities for Accenture to support HHSC with compliance strengthening and audit preparation services.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review OIG work plan items for potential HHSC compliance implications

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline acceleration
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropping 3.1% YoY signals potential redetermination challenges and system strain requiring intervention.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Propose enrollment stabilization and redetermination process optimization services.

riskOpportunity.label: Quality performance gap
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks 30th nationally with 62/100 quality score, creating improvement mandate and consulting opportunities.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop comprehensive quality improvement roadmap targeting top-tier performance metrics.

riskOpportunity.label: HHSC procurement pipeline
riskOpportunity.description: Active Texas HHSC procurement portal indicates upcoming contract opportunities but competitive landscape risk.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Establish dedicated HHSC procurement tracking and response capability.

riskOpportunity.label: HCBS quality mandates
riskOpportunity.description: New 2028 Medicaid HCBS quality measure requirements create compliance risk but also transformation opportunity.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position as lead HCBS quality transformation partner before competitors mobilize.

riskOpportunity.label: OIG oversight intensification
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHS OIG Medicaid portfolio oversight creates compliance scrutiny risk but positions Accenture's governance expertise.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Enhance compliance monitoring and audit readiness capabilities immediately.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care optimization
riskOpportunity.description: Texas's 93.3% managed care penetration above national average creates efficiency pressure but innovation opportunity.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop advanced managed care analytics and population health management solutions.

riskOpportunity.label: Per-enrollee cost efficiency
riskOpportunity.description: Texas $2,988 per-enrollee cost near national average suggests budget optimization pressure and value-based opportunities.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Propose value-based care transformation and cost optimization analytics platform.

riskOpportunity.label: Multi-state competitive positioning
riskOpportunity.description: Texas as 4th largest Medicaid program creates high-visibility competitive battlefield with national consulting firms.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Establish Texas as flagship Medicaid modernization showcase to win other large state competitions.
